name: Vitamin Sea
description:
What happens when a food that is part livelihood, part cuisine, and part history slowly disappears?

Join artist Parag Tandel for a deep dive into his exhibit ‘_How To Cook Bombay Duck In Various Ways?_’ 

The Bombay duck (a fish locally called Bombil) is a staple food of the Mumbai fisherfolk community, the Kolis. In recent years, however, development projects and pollution have lowered the catch, affecting both the livelihood and culinary habits of the Koli community.

Drawing on his practice as a visual autoethnographer of the Koli community, he reflects on themes of loss, identity, and memory. Through oral histories, material culture, and socially engaged art practices, this talk explores the colonial histories of Mumbai’s fisherfolk and the changing relationship between the city, the sea, and its original inhabitants.

name: Creating a Living Museum of Memory
description:
Questioning the idea of the museum as a body, artist Parag Tandel will reflect on building The Tandel Fund of Archives, a living museum of memory of the Koli community (an Indigenous fishing tribe and the original inhabitants of Mumbai). He collaborates with more than two hundred villages to archive narratives of their Indigenous knowledge of the ocean and other biophilic practices in and around Mumbai.

Blurring the boundaries between contemporary art, archives, and community practice, this workshop explores how museums can become gathering points where stories and memories come together. Through this approach, the archive shifts from being a static repository to a dynamic space for dialogue, reflection, and collective imagination.

name: Spinfinity: Spinning Maths with Hula Hoops
description:
Can we understand math through hula hooping? 

A lot of mathematical ideas are three-dimensional and require imagination. But the way we usually study it is two-dimensional: on paper, on blackboards, and on screens. This workshop explores mathematics out of the blackboard and into the body, with hula hoops. We will explore angles, shapes, circle properties, and tangents with hula hooping games and tricks.

name: A Bite of CALORIE: Hindi Walkthrough
description: Join the Science Gallery Bengaluru team for a closer look at our new exhibition season, CALORIE, one bite at a time. This walkthrough, conducted in Hindi, invites you to reflect on the ideas of food, nutrition, and energy. As we move through the exhibition, the team will unpack the intimate, and often complicated, relationships we share with food, nourishment, and the systems that sustain us.

name: A Bite of CALORIE: Tamil Walkthrough
description: Join the Science Gallery Bengaluru team for a closer look at our new exhibition season, CALORIE, one bite at a time. This walkthrough, conducted in Tamil, invites you to reflect on the ideas of food, nutrition, and energy. As we move through the exhibition, the team will unpack the intimate, and often complicated, relationships we share with food, nourishment, and the systems that sustain us.

name: Remembering the 1943 Bengal Famine: Hunger, Empathy and Violence
description:
How does a society remember a disaster like a famine?

In this talk Diya Gupta will look at archival letters, 1940s photographs, and artwork to examine how we might study the man-made 1943 Bengal Famine particularly through the eyes of the witnesses of the calamity. The talk will go beyond trying to establish what caused the famine and focus on the emotional responses of those who saw extreme hunger escalate before their eyes, and those who heard about it from afar. Going beyond Bengal, she will explore how food shortages affected large parts of South India too. And finally, with thoughts on how we might remember the famine today, she reflects on how that might recalibrate our own relationship with food and those we love.

name: Yele Oota
description:
When did nourishment become a commodity?

Explore this idea through the lens of the contemporary dance piece ‘Yele Oota’ performed by the Vishwakiran Nambi Dance Company. The performance explores humanity's most fundamental relationship, our connection with food and the act of sharing nourishment.

Through seven dancers and movement narratives, the choreography shifts between depicting resource accumulation and the gentle art of giving. Drawing from traditions where feeding others was considered the highest service, the piece celebrates the timeless wisdom of community kitchens and shared meals.

Using edible props that transform throughout the performance, ‘Yele Oota’ builds toward a powerful revelation: generosity is not merely a choice but a survival strategy for human civilisation.

name: Reimagining Oral Histories As Narratives
description:
How do we record peoples memories and personal experience?

Join Shreeya, Kamakshi and Pooja as they walk participants through the nuances, documentation processes and recording of oral history. Oral history is a research methodology that treats the spoken word as a vital and authentic source of historical evidence. It is the systematic collection of living people’s testimonies, memories, and personal experiences within a larger social context, through recorded interviews. 

In this workshop, discover its pivotal role in preserving the voices of underrepresented groups and filling gaps where written documentation is lacking. Learn how oral history can help us engage with research through the arts.

name: What's For Dinner & Who's Making It?
description:
Whose labour runs the kitchen?

Artists Shreya and Kamakshi host a conversation that looks at the disproportionate and invisible labour that goes into the making and management of food in homes and the deep seated gender bias that shapes the functioning of a kitchen in most familial settings. This talk questions the roles and responsibilities of an individual around nutrition and care giving of the self and the other. This talk will explore the idea of the making, distributing and consuming food, across the spectrum of genders, caste and class.

name: Mise en Play: Play as a Creative Tool
description:
Every day, without thinking about it, you make sense of your surroundings through what you eat, what you hear, what your body tells you. 

We are all meaning-making creatures, and our earliest instinct was to do this through play. Play is a creative act in itself. Growing up, play was how we explored, expressed, and understood the world. Somewhere along the way, we stopped. Here is an invitation to start again.

This programme takes you through CALORIE as a space where many different perspectives and processes come together, and in doing so, we begin to see creativity not as a skill but as a practice. That practice is what we invite you into.

Expect hands-on activities that engage all your senses. You need no background in art or design, only an openness to notice, engage, and explore. Leave with a new way of observing your environment and documenting your experiences.

Join experimentors Nirupama and Shreya for a programme that treats creativity as something that belongs to everyone.
